architecture.summary: This paper proposes an evaluation pipeline for activation steering methods (representation engineering). The pipeline takes a base LLM (e.g., Llama 2 7B Chat) and an activation steering intervention (e.g., CAA or ITI), generates behavior-testing queries with positive and negative continuations, records token log-likelihoods from both baseline and intervened models, renormalizes likelihoods, and visualizes/quantifies the separation between positive and negative sample likelihoods. The core mechanism is comparative likelihood analysis: effective interventions lower negative-sample log-likelihoods and raise positive ones, measured over increasing sample set sizes (top 25%, 50%, 75% by baseline likelihood).

biblio.title: Towards Reliable Evaluation of Behavior Steering Interventions in LLMs

claims.statement: Evaluation pipelines for representation engineering methods have primarily relied on subjective demonstrations, instead of quantitative, objective metrics

Prompt format significantly influences the success of interventions: CAA successfully induced corrigible behavior in a multiple-choice format but failed to elicit the desired behavior in an open-ended generation setting
Some interventions are less effective than previously reported

claims.dataset: 50 open-ended prompts from Panickssery et al. with GPT-4 generated continuations
claims.statement: For ITI on truthfulness (top 25%), log-likelihood of positive samples increases by 0.08 on average and negative samples decrease by 0.08

claims.dataset: 50 open-ended prompts from Panickssery et al. with GPT-4 generated continuations
claims.statement: CAA with negated hallucination steering vector at top 50% shows metric score of 0.07 for negative samples while positive samples increase only by 0.02

claims.dataset: 50 open-ended prompts from Panickssery et al. with GPT-4 generated continuations
claims.statement: CAA with hallucination steering vector yields inconsistent results with no clear pattern in raising the likelihood of untruthful sentences

claims.dataset: 50 open-ended prompts from Panickssery et al. with GPT-4 generated continuations
claims.statement: Corrigible CAA shows erratic likelihood shifts similar to hallucination CAA

claims.dataset: 50 open-ended prompts from Panickssery et al. with GPT-4 generated continuations
claims.statement: Sycophancy CAA has virtually no effect on model preferences between hallucinated and truthful sentences
